planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in shade. The silver-spotted leaves complement large coral-pink flowers that open early in spring. Keep evenly watered and shear tired foliage in summer to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist soil in shade. The narrow silver-spotted leaves back an abundant, long-lasting show of magenta to coral-red flowers in early to mid spring. Deadhead and water well to prolong bloom and protect the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in rich, evenly moist soil in shade. The upright leaves are heavily silvered, nearly white, and stand up well through the season. Raspberry-pink flowers appear over the pale foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in shade. The silver-spotted leaves set off large white flowers that brighten shady spring borders. Keep the soil moist and remove faded stems to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn or give a cold-moist chilling before spring sowing.

directions: Surface-sow the feathery seed and press it lightly into gritty, sharply drained soil, leaving it uncovered since light aids sprouting. Keep the crown dry and give the plant full sun; it resents being moved once the deep taproot forms. Nodding bell flowers open in early spring, followed by silky seed heads that are ornamental in their own right.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed for several weeks or sow fresh in fall for spring sprouting.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface of a lean, gritty mix and keep it just moist until it germinates. Plant in a sunny rock garden or border edge with excellent drainage, and avoid disturbing the taproot. Deep wine-red cups appear on hairy stems in early spring above a rosette of fern-like leaves.

planting.note: Fresh seed germinates best; older seed benefits from a cold-moist period.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of well-drained soil and firm it gently, leaving it exposed to light. Site it in full sun with sharp drainage and let the soil dry between waterings once established. Pure white bells with golden central bosses open in spring, giving way to fluffy silver seed plumes.

planting.note: Sow in fall or stratify before a spring start for reliable germination.

directions: Barely cover the hairy seed and keep the tray in bright, cool conditions until seedlings appear. Grow on in gritty soil in full sun and set young plants out before the taproot lengthens. Rich red flowers nod on downy stems in early spring, an old German selection prized for its saturated color.

planting.note: Chill seed a few weeks before spring sowing, or sow fresh in autumn.

directions: Sow on the surface of a free-draining mix and leave uncovered, keeping it lightly moist until germination. This strain gives semi-double, laciniated flowers in a mix of red, purple, pink, and white, so grow several for the full range. Fringed blooms open in early spring above finely cut foliage in a sunny, well-drained spot.

planting.note: Named clone grown from root cuttings or careful division rather than seed.

directions: Set potted plants into gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun, keeping the crown at or just above grade. Because this is a vegetatively propagated selection, it comes true only from root cuttings, not seed. Large, deep crimson-red bells open in early spring and hold their intense color well against the fern-like leaves.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn or stratify before spring for best results.

directions: Surface-sow and press seed lightly into gritty compost, leaving it uncovered for light. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage and disturb the roots as little as possible. Soft pink cups on hairy stems open in early spring above a low rosette, followed by silky seed heads.

planting.note: Autumn sowing or a cold-moist chill helps this selection germinate.

directions: Press the feathery seed onto the surface and keep it cool and lightly moist until it sprouts. Plant in a sunny, sharply drained bed and let the taproot settle undisturbed. Violet-blue bells nod on downy stems in early spring, a classic blue-toned German selection.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall; the Baltic race tolerates lean, alkaline soils.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a gritty, limy mix and leave it uncovered. This compact island subspecies wants full sun and very sharp drainage, thriving on thin calcareous ground. Small blue-violet bells appear in early spring, later than the type, above finely divided leaves.

planting.note: Fresh autumn seed or a cold chill improves germination of this larger form.

directions: Surface-sow and keep just moist in bright, cool conditions until seedlings emerge. This Central European subspecies bears noticeably larger flowers and wants full sun with excellent drainage. Broad violet bells open in early spring, often before the leaves fully expand.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify prairie seed for a month before spring sowing.

directions: Give the seed a cold-moist period, then surface-sow into lean, gritty, well-drained soil. This North American and Eurasian prairie native is drought-tolerant once established and hates wet winter crowns. Pale lavender to blue cups hug the ground in very early spring, before the finely cut leaves are fully grown.

planting.note: Stratify seed cold and moist, then surface-sow in early spring.

directions: Sow the fluffy seed on the surface of a sharply drained, gritty mix and leave it uncovered. This hardy North American prairie plant needs full sun and dry summer soil, and it resents transplanting once the taproot forms. Silvery-lavender cups open close to the ground as the snow retreats, followed by feathery seed heads.

planting.note: Sow fresh in autumn or cold-stratify before a spring start.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty, free-draining soil and firm gently, leaving seed exposed to light. This slender European species prefers full sun and dry, sandy ground. Nodding, bell-shaped flowers in dusky violet dangle from downy stems in mid spring, more demure than the common pasque flower.

planting.note: Cold-moist chill seed a few weeks, then surface-sow in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a lean, gritty mix and keep it cool until it germinates. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and dry summer conditions. Small, deeply pigmented dark violet bells nod on hairy stems in mid spring, giving this subspecies its dusky name.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall or stratify before a spring sowing.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty alpine soil and leave uncovered, keeping it lightly moist until sprouting. This compact species from the southern Alps wants full sun and very sharp drainage in a scree or rock garden. Deep violet, nodding bells appear early on short hairy stems, close to the emerging foliage.

planting.note: Fresh seed in autumn germinates best; otherwise cold-stratify.

directions: Press the woolly seed onto a gritty, free-draining surface and leave it exposed to light. This alpine species is notably hairy and wants full sun with excellent drainage. Large, silky violet cups open in mid to late spring, densely clothed in silvery hairs that catch the light.

planting.note: Slow to establish; cold-stratify seed and be patient with germination.

directions: Surface-sow and give the seed a cold-moist period, expecting slow and uneven germination. This mountain species prefers full sun to light shade with cool, moist but well-drained soil. Upright white flowers, often flushed blue on the reverse, open in late spring and give way to spectacular silvery seed plumes.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; expect slow, staggered germination.

directions: Surface-sow onto a gritty, humus-rich mix and chill it before moving to warmth. This yellow-flowered alpine race favors acidic mountain soils in full sun to light shade with steady moisture. Soft sulphur-yellow blooms open in late spring, later ripening into fluffy silver seed heads.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing.

directions: Press seed onto a gritty, free-draining surface and leave it uncovered for light. This species from France and Spain wants full sun and very sharp drainage. Deep brownish-red to dark maroon bells nod on hairy stems in mid spring above finely dissected leaves.

planting.note: Fussy alpine; sow fresh seed and expect slow germination.

directions: Surface-sow fresh seed onto a lean, gritty alpine mix and keep it cool and evenly moist. This high-mountain gem needs full sun, perfect drainage, and protection from winter wet at the crown. Nodding white flowers, gold-centered and washed violet-blue outside with a shaggy silver ruff of hairs, open in very early spring.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; this subalpine native germinates slowly.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist chill, then surface-sow into gritty, well-drained soil. This western North American mountain species likes full sun and cool, moisture-retentive but freely draining ground. White or cream cups open just after snowmelt, followed by tall, shaggy seed heads nicknamed hippie-on-a-stick.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn or stratify before spring sowing.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty, free-draining soil and keep it cool until it germinates. This East Asian species wants full sun and sharp drainage. Deeply nodding, dark reddish-purple bells hang from hairy stems in spring above finely cut, silvery foliage.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then surface-sow in early spring.

directions: Press seed onto a gritty surface and leave uncovered, keeping the tray cool. This Siberian and Mongolian steppe species is very cold-hardy and drought-tolerant, wanting full sun and lean soil. Bright violet-blue cups open in spring above finely divided, ferny leaves.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed in spring; tolerates a range of well-drained soils.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty, free-draining soil and keep just moist until seedlings appear. This East Asian species, long used in traditional medicine, wants full sun and sharp drainage. Purple to violet bells open in spring on hairy stems, followed by the characteristic feathery seed heads.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed before a spring surface sowing.

directions: Scatter seed on a gritty, well-drained surface and leave it exposed to light. This Caucasus and Turkey native wants full sun and sharp drainage in a rock garden. Small, nodding yellow bells open in mid spring above finely cut, hairy foliage.

planting.note: Very cold-hardy; stratify seed before spring sowing.

directions: Surface-sow onto lean, gritty soil and keep the tray cool until germination. This species from northeastern Asia is extremely cold-tolerant and wants full sun with dry summer soil. Violet to reddish-purple bells nod on hairy stems in spring above ferny leaves.

planting.note: Cold-stratify steppe seed, then surface-sow in spring.

directions: Press seed onto a gritty surface and leave uncovered, keeping conditions cool. This Central Asian steppe species is drought-tolerant and cold-hardy, wanting full sun and lean, sharply drained soil. Small violet-blue bells open in spring above finely divided, hairy leaves.

planting.note: Sow fresh or stratified seed onto a well-drained surface in spring.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty, free-draining soil and keep it lightly moist until it sprouts. This Korean endemic wants full sun and sharp drainage. Nodding reddish-purple bells open in spring on densely hairy stems, followed by silvery, feathery seed heads.

planting.note: Compact seed strain bred for uniform, early first-year bloom.

directions: Surface-sow onto a gritty mix and leave uncovered, keeping it lightly moist and warm until germination. This tidy commercial strain flowers earlier and more compactly than older seedlings, ideal for containers and border fronts. Blue-violet bells open in early spring above neat, finely cut foliage.

planting.note: Uniform, compact seed strain that can bloom in its first year.

directions: Sow on the surface of a free-draining mix, press lightly, and keep warm until seedlings appear. This compact strain gives early, uniform flowers well suited to pots and the front of a sunny bed. Rich dark-red bells nod above finely dissected leaves in early spring.

planting.note: Direct sow after the soil warms, or start indoors following a few weeks of cold-moist stratification.

directions: Barely cover the tiny seeds and keep the surface moist until sprouts appear, since light aids germination. Give this drought-loving prairie native lean, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering once established. Look for the tall columnar central cone ringed by drooping yellow petals, which resembles a sombrero and often blooms the first summer from an early sowing.

planting.note: Sow into warm garden soil in spring, or start early indoors after cold stratification.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil surface and keep it lightly moist, as it needs light to sprout. Plant in gritty, well-drained ground and let it dry between waterings to prevent flop and root rot. This naturally occurring form carries rich mahogany-red petals, often edged in gold, that droop below the tall central cone from early to late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after frost danger, or start indoors early for first-year bloom.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and tamp lightly, keeping it moist until germination since light triggers sprouting. This compact selection stays shorter and bushier than the species, making it useful at the front of a dry border. Deep red petals tipped with yellow surround the raised cone through summer; shear spent flowers to encourage a fresh flush.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring into warm, well-drained soil after light stratification.

directions: Sow shallowly into lean, gritty soil and water sparingly once seedlings establish. This bushy, many-branched southwestern native forms short cones with a few small yellow-to-brownish petals rather than long rays. It thrives on heat and drought, blooming through summer and reseeding gently where conditions suit it.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring into warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lightly moist until germination, since it responds to light. Native to Texas and the south-central plains, it carries long bare flower stalks topped by a dark cone ringed with yellow, often brown-blotched, drooping petals. Grow it in dry, sunny sites where it blooms across spring and summer and needs little supplemental water.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly and keep the bed evenly moist until sprouts emerge. This heat-loving species from Mexico and the far southern plains prefers sharp drainage and full sun. It produces columnar cones with reflexed yellow petals through the warm season and resents wet, heavy soils in winter.

planting.note: Set divisions in moist, humus-rich soil after the ground warms in spring.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil surface in deep, moisture-retentive ground that never dries out. The large horse-chestnut-shaped leaves scorch in dry wind, so shelter the plant and mulch heavily to keep roots cool. Creamy white plumes rise on tall stems in early summer above the bold foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist woodland soil with shelter from wind.

directions: Site in deep, damp, leafy soil where the jagged five-lobed leaves can spread without drying out. New foliage emerges bronze, greens through summer, and often flushes coppery again in autumn. Airy creamy-white flower heads appear in early summer on tall branching stems.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a cool, moist border or streamside planting.

directions: Plant in rich, damp soil in partial shade, keeping the crown at ground level. The elder-like pinnate leaves prefer steady moisture and dislike hot, dry exposure. Flat sprays of creamy pink flowers open above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, moisture-retentive woodland spot.

directions: Grow in deep, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist in light shade. Shelter the broad divided leaves from drying wind to prevent scorch. Loose sprays of creamy flowers rise on branched stems in summer.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring in cool, damp, fertile soil.

directions: Plant in moisture-retentive ground with the crown just below the surface and mulch to conserve water. The bold palmate leaves emerge with bronze tints and expand to a striking mound. Tall pink-tinged plumes open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, reliably moist soil.

directions: Plant in damp, humus-rich soil in light shade, keeping the roots cool and evenly watered. Young leaves emerge purple-bronze before maturing to green, giving strong spring color. Bright pink flower plumes stand well above the foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set divisions in spring in moist, fertile ground with afternoon shade.

directions: Plant in consistently moist soil where the chocolate-brown new foliage can be admired against lighter neighbors. Keep well watered to hold the dark leaf color and prevent scorch. Deep rose-red flower spikes rise above the foliage in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into a cool, moisture-retentive border.

directions: Grow in rich, damp soil in partial shade with steady summer moisture. The pinnate foliage forms a bold clump beneath the flower stems. Soft pale-pink to creamy plumes open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in dappled light.

directions: Plant in damp, humus-rich ground and keep the soil moist through the growing season. The dark-tinted foliage sets off the flowers well in a bog garden or streamside. Fiery red flower spikes rise on strong stems in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, moist woodland soil.

directions: Site in cool, damp, fertile soil with shelter from drying wind. The large palmate leaves flush a rich bronze on emergence for strong spring interest. Creamy-white plumes rise on tall stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, leafy soil in partial shade.

directions: Grow in deep, consistently damp soil where the jagged leaves can develop their red-bronze spring tones. Keep sheltered and mulched to prevent leaf scorch in dry spells. Creamy flower heads open in early summer, and foliage often reddens again in autumn.

planting.note: Set divisions in spring in cool, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Plant in damp woodland soil in light shade with shelter from wind. New foliage emerges warm brown before greening, adding depth to spring plantings. Airy white-cream flower panicles appear on tall stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist soil in partial shade.

directions: Plant in damp, humus-rich soil and keep evenly watered to hold the deep bronze leaf color. This is a more compact selection, useful at the front of a shaded bog border. Creamy flower sprays rise above the crinkled foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moist, fertile soil with room to spread.

directions: Give this vigorous hybrid deep, damp soil and space to build a large, bold clump. Keep well watered and sheltered to support the substantial foliage. Tall pink-flushed flower plumes rise strongly above the leaves in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in reliably moist, fertile ground.

directions: Plant in cool, damp soil in dappled shade and keep the roots moist all season. Dark stems and bronze-tinted foliage give a moody backdrop to the flower spikes. Upright red-tinged plumes open above the foliage in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist streamside or bog border.

directions: Grow in rich, damp soil in partial shade with steady moisture through summer. The elder-like pinnate leaves take on reddish tints, especially on emergence. Flat creamy-pink flower sprays open on branching stems in early summer.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring in a sunny, open spot.

directions: Plant this compact selection at the front of a border and firm the soil around the roots. Its short, sturdy stems rarely flop and need no staking. Golden-yellow flowers smother the tidy mound from midsummer well into fall.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in full sun with good air movement.

directions: Give this hybrid room to form its broad, domed mound and avoid crowding it, which helps keep the narrow foliage healthy. It resists the leaf spot that troubles many black-eyed Susans. Golden daisies with reddish halos bloom for many weeks from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors eight weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom.

directions: Sow the seed on the surface under bright light and transplant seedlings out after frost danger passes. This vigorous selection carries very large single golden flowers up to nine inches across. Blooms appear from early summer until frost and make excellent long-stemmed cut flowers.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out once the weather settles.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and moist, then grow the seedlings on in bright light. This award-winner shows golden petals shading to lemon tips around a distinctive green central cone. Flowers open all summer and are striking in bouquets.

planting.note: Start indoors for the longest season of bloom.

directions: Barely cover the seed and provide warmth and light until germination. Plants bear double and semi-double flowers in a warm mix of gold, bronze, mahogany, and rust. Blooms carry from summer to frost and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Start indoors under lights eight weeks ahead of planting out.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep warm, then transplant after frost. This unusual selection blooms in deep cherry-red tones around a dark chocolate cone. Deadhead regularly to keep flowers coming from summer through fall.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and move out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix and leave uncovered, as light speeds germination. Golden-yellow petals surround a soft green central cone rather than the usual dark one. Flowers bloom from midsummer to frost and are prized for arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors for containers and edging.

directions: Surface-sow and keep bright and warm until seedlings appear. This dwarf strain stays compact and neat, making it ideal for pots and the front of beds. Golden daisies with dark centers cover the little plants all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep it lightly moist under good light. This compact selection produces fully double and semi-double golden pompon flowers. Bloom continues from summer to frost on bushy, well-branched plants.

planting.note: Start indoors eight weeks before setting out.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep warm until it sprouts. Large single flowers open in shades from gold through bronze, rust, and deep red around a dark cone. Blooms carry from summer into fall and are good for cutting.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors for earlier flowers.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press it in without covering. This mix blooms in golden, bronze, and mahogany bicolors on shorter, well-branched stems. Flowers appear from midsummer through fall and attract bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Start indoors for this quilled novelty.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and bright to germinate. The narrow, rolled petals give the flowers a spidery, quilled look in gold, bronze, and rust tones. Blooms last from summer to frost and are unusual in arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it moist under light. Large golden flowers carry a bold mahogany ring around the dark central cone. Plants bloom heavily from summer to frost and stand up well in the garden.

planting.note: Start indoors for these compact double flowers.

directions: Surface-sow and grow seedlings on in bright, warm conditions. This dwarf selection bears fully double golden flowers that look almost like small dahlias. Compact plants bloom from summer to frost and suit containers well.

planting.note: Start indoors for large bicolor blooms.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and evenly moist. Big flowers show gold petals brushed and ringed with warm bronze and mahogany around a dark eye. Well-branched plants bloom freely from summer to frost and cut well.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it under bright light until it germinates. This old favorite bears large deep golden-orange flowers with a nearly black central cone. Compact, uniform plants flower from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors for these very short, early plants.

directions: Press the seed onto moist mix and leave it uncovered under light. This dwarf strain blooms very early in a mix of gold, lemon, bronze, and mahogany tones. The knee-high plants flower from summer to frost and thrive in pots.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and bright until germination. Large single flowers show gold outer tips over a broad mahogany zone around a dark cone. Compact plants bloom heavily from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Give this stately selection deep, fertile soil and steady moisture, and stake it in exposed sites. Also sold as Autumn Sun, it forms a strong upright clump over the season. Large single yellow flowers with green cones tower over the border from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this shorter double form where the soil stays reasonably moist through summer. Its stems are sturdier and more compact than the wild species. Fully double lemon-yellow pompon flowers open from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring for a cold period.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and let winter cold do the stratifying. This selection bears masses of small bicolor daisies in gold tipped with rusty orange around a dark cone. The tall, branching plants bloom from late summer to frost and are great for cutting.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this distinctive selection in full sun and give the tall stems room; a light early trim keeps them bushier. Each golden flower has narrow, rolled, quilled petals for an unusual pinwheel look. Bloom lasts through late summer and into fall.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this more compact quilled selection a sunny, open position. Its narrow, rolled golden petals form the same pinwheel flowers on a shorter, tidier plant. Bloom carries from late summer into autumn and attracts many pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in full sun.

directions: Set plants where the soil holds moisture and give the tall stems support in windy spots. The glossy foliage gives this southeastern native its name. Large yellow flowers with green, cone-like centers rise on tall stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: Give this prairie native full sun and well-drained soil, and it will tolerate heat and drought once established. Large golden flowers surround a tall, dome-shaped brown cone. Bloom comes in mid to late summer and the seed heads feed birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil and full sun to light shade.

directions: Set plants in a moisture-retentive spot; they tolerate a little shade in hot regions. This unusual coneflower has nearly petal-less flowers that are just tall black-green cones. The striking seed heads are excellent in fresh and dried arrangements.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Give this drought-tough native full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Its narrow, hairy leaves and slender stems suit dry meadows and rock gardens. Golden daisies with dark cones bloom over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth and keep watered while establishing. This free-flowering variety forms a bushy clump that blooms very heavily. Golden daisies with dark cones cover the plants from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in full sun.

directions: Set plants in fertile, well-drained soil and water through their first summer. This selection starts flowering earlier than most and reblooms if deadheaded. Golden daisies with dark cones cover the compact clumps from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock in spring at the front of a bed.

directions: Plant this dwarf selection where its short stature can be seen and keep it watered while rooting in. The compact mounds stay tidy and need no staking. Golden daisies with dark cones cover the little plants from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it lightly into moist soil. These large-flowered strains carry big golden, bronze, and mahogany daisies, some banded around the cone. Blooms open from summer to frost and make long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Start indoors for this dwarf container type.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep warm and bright until seedlings appear. This dwarf selection stays low and bears flowers in warm gold and mahogany bicolors. Perfect for pots and edging, it blooms all summer on neat little plants.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and keep it under bright light. This compact, well-branched selection was bred for uniform habit and heavy flowering. Golden daisies with dark cones cover the tidy plants from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and leave it uncovered under good light. Golden-yellow petals surround a soft green cone instead of the usual dark eye. Flowers bloom from midsummer to frost and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Start indoors for these soft muted colors.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow the seedlings on in warmth and bright light. This strain blooms in soft, dusky tones of rose, caramel, bronze, and antique gold, often bicolored. Flowers carry from summer to frost and are lovely in bouquets.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or set out plants once hard frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it evenly moist at room temperature until sprouts appear. Give plants lean, well-drained soil and full sun, and avoid rich feeding that causes floppy growth. Shear the spent flower spikes after the first flush to trigger a second wave of bloom from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring after the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant so the crown sits level with the soil and space clumps for good airflow. Prized for its near-black flower stems topped with violet-blue whorls, it needs sharp drainage and full sun. Cut back after flowering for repeat spikes; divide congested clumps every three or four years in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the ground is workable and frost danger is easing.

directions: Set plants into lean, freely draining soil and firm them in gently. This 1997 Perennial Plant of the Year carries deep indigo spikes very early in the season. Deadhead the fading stems to keep flowers coming into summer and shear the clump back hard in late fall.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after the last hard frost.

directions: Space plants a foot or more apart in full sun and water sparingly once established. This compact classic bears dense purple-blue spikes that draw bees and butterflies. Trim off finished flowers to prompt rebloom, then cut the whole plant back after the main flush fades.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep warm and moist until germination. This seed-grown strain often blooms its first summer with violet-blue spikes on tidy plants. Deadhead regularly and give it lean soil and full sun for the best flowering.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or set out plants after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and provide steady warmth for good germination. This pink-flowered counterpart to Blue Queen forms rosy-purple spikes above gray-green foliage. Shear the plant after its first flush and it will bloom again through summer in full sun.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery stock in spring.

directions: Set plants in gritty, well-drained soil and keep them on the dry side. Loose spikes of rosy-lilac flowers rise on branching stems in early summer. Cut back spent stalks to encourage a lighter second flush and improve the plant's form.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the soil warms.

directions: Give this cultivar full sun and lean, well-drained ground. It bears deep violet spikes on reddish stems very early in the season. Deadhead to prolong bloom and shear the clump back in autumn once flowering ends.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: Space these compact plants closely for a tidy front-of-border ribbon. Rose-pink spikes cover the dwarf mounds in early summer. Trim off the faded flowers to keep the plants blooming and neat through the season.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant this very dwarf selection at the front edge of a sunny bed or in a container. It forms low cushions smothered in violet-blue spikes despite its tiny stature. Shear lightly after the first bloom to keep the mound compact and flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Give this compact variety full sun and free-draining soil. Violet-blue spikes with reddish calyces cover the plant over a long early-summer season. Deadhead often and cut back after the main flush to keep it blooming.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants in a sunny, well-drained spot and water only when dry. This selection offers clear, true-blue spikes without the purple tones of many sages. Shear spent flowers for repeat bloom and trim the whole plant back in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once soil warms.

directions: Plant this white-flowered form in full sun with sharp drainage. Its pure white spikes make a cool contrast to the blue and purple sages. Deadhead to prolong flowering and shear the clump back after the first heavy bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Give this dwarf, deep-violet cultivar full sun and lean soil. Compact spikes of rich purple flowers rise above tidy foliage in early summer. Cut back after blooming to keep the mound dense and to encourage a second show.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Set this vigorous selection in full sun with well-drained soil. It produces long violet-blue spikes on strong stems well into summer. Shear after the first flush and divide clumps every few years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: Grow this rosy-pink selection in full sun and freely draining soil. Its warm pink spikes rise on well-branched stems in early summer. Deadhead to keep the flowers coming and cut the plant back hard in autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery stock in spring.

directions: This sterile hybrid is grown from divisions rather than seed and thrives in full sun. It bears dense violet-purple spikes with persistent reddish bracts after the petals drop. Shear the clump after flowering to prompt rebloom and to keep it neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost has passed.

directions: Set this dwarf selection near the front of a sunny border. It carries deep pink-purple spikes on compact, well-branched mounds. Trim spent flowers to keep it blooming from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Grow this early, compact cultivar in full sun and lean soil. It opens deep violet-blue spikes noticeably ahead of many other sages. Deadhead promptly to extend the display and shear back after the main flush.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year bloom.

directions: Sow this seed-raised strain indoors and barely cover the seed. It forms uniform, compact plants topped with deep blue spikes in the first season. Deadhead to keep it flowering and give it full sun with sharp drainage.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or set out plants after frost.

directions: Start this compact seed strain indoors under warmth for first-year flowering. It bears violet-blue spikes on very short, well-branched plants ideal for edging or pots. Shear lightly after bloom to keep the mound tidy.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: Set this large-flowered selection in full sun with good drainage. It is noted for unusually big violet-blue blooms on a compact, spreading plant. Deadhead to keep flowers coming through summer and cut back in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Grow this soft-pink counterpart to East Friesland in full sun. Slender spikes of pale pink flowers rise above compact gray-green foliage. Deadhead often and shear back after the first flush for repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: This sport of Caradonna keeps the dark stems but pairs them with soft pink flowers. Plant in full sun with lean, well-drained soil for strong upright spikes. Cut back spent stems to encourage a second flush later in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Give this free-flowering selection full sun and well-drained soil. It sends up violet-blue spikes over a long season with good rebloom. Deadhead regularly and shear the whole clump back after the heaviest flush.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or sow outdoors in spring.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and moist to germinate. This European meadow native forms basal rosettes topped by tall blue-violet flower stems in early summer. Cut back after bloom and it may flower again; it also self-sows in open, sunny ground.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set this pink-flowered meadow sage in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Airy spikes of soft pink blooms rise above the rosette in early summer. Deadhead to encourage rebloom and divide clumps in spring if they crowd.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Grow this lilac-sage in full sun with well-drained soil. It bears whorled spikes of dusky purple flowers with dark calyces over a long season. Shear off spent stems to keep it blooming and cut back in late fall.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or potted stock in spring.

directions: This popular Russian sage selection has deeply cut foliage and large violet-blue panicles. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and avoid rich or wet soil. Prune the woody base back hard each spring to keep it dense and upright.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: This compact Russian sage stays about half the height of the standard form. Grow it in full sun with lean, dry soil for sturdy, self-supporting stems. Cut it back to a few inches in early spring and enjoy lavender-blue haze from midsummer on.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after frost.

directions: This selection carries rich cherry-red flowers that draw hummingbirds all season. Give it full sun and gritty, well-drained soil, and go easy on water. Trim it back after heavy flushes to keep it compact and blooming into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in mild regions.

directions: This shrubby sage is famous for bicolor red-and-white flowers that shift with the temperature. Grow it in full sun with free-draining soil and light watering. Shear it back in midsummer if it sprawls, and it will bloom from late spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers or potted stock in spring after all frost.

directions: This tuberous anise-scented sage bears cobalt-blue flowers held in near-black calyces on tall stems. Give it full sun to light shade and moderately moist, fertile soil. In colder zones lift the tubers before hard frost or mulch heavily; it flowers from summer until frost and draws hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after frost.

directions: This shrubby hybrid sage bears deep velvety purple flowers over a long season. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and water sparingly once settled in. Trim spent growth to keep it tidy and blooming from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or sow in spring.

directions: This clump-forming species sends up tall bare stems that nod with clusters of soft blue-violet flowers. Grow it in full sun and well-drained soil from a low basal rosette of leaves. Deadhead after bloom in early summer and give it room to show off its airy flower stems.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and transplant after it passes.

directions: Start seed indoors under warmth and press it lightly into the surface, as light aids germination. This mealycup sage bears slender violet-blue spikes on silvery stems all summer and is grown as an annual in cold regions. Deadhead to keep it blooming and give it full sun with average moisture.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or sow outdoors in spring.

directions: This clumping species forms large coarse basal leaves and open spikes of pinkish-violet flowers. It tolerates light shade better than most sages and prefers reasonably moist, well-drained soil. Cut back spent flower stems after the early-summer bloom to tidy the plant and prompt fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frost.

directions: Part of the dwarf Sensation series, this cultivar forms compact mounds of deep blue spikes. Plant it in full sun with lean, well-drained soil for the front of a border. Shear it after the first flush to keep it neat and to bring on more flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: This selection bears wine-rose flowers on slender spikes above gray-green foliage. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and modest water. Deadhead to extend the bloom and shear the clump back after the main early-summer flush.

planting.note: Set out divisions or sow in spring into moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Grow in full sun to light shade in humus-rich soil kept evenly moist. The arching stems carry fuzzy pink caterpillar-like flower heads and may lean, so a neighboring support helps. Bloom comes in mid to late summer with a soft, nodding habit.

planting.note: Sow or divide in spring into rich, damp soil in an open site.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist soil where the tall stems get full light. Its finely divided foliage forms a wide base beneath dangling flower spikes. Slender pink to purple bottlebrush blooms sway on the ends of arching stems from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and replant into moist, well-worked soil.

directions: Set divisions at the depth they grew before, in full sun and reliably moist soil. Blue-green scalloped foliage makes a tidy mound early in the year. Plump crimson-purple flower heads appear notably early, often opening in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Give it a cool root run in humus-rich, consistently damp soil with some afternoon shade in hot regions. Grey-green ferny leaves form a low rosette. Long, drooping mauve-pink tassels dangle from the stems through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Divide or plant out in spring into good garden soil.

directions: Grow in full sun to light shade in soil that holds some moisture. The soft grey-green pinnate leaves make an attractive foil for the flowers. Pale nodding flower spikes with prominent stamens open through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Lift and divide established clumps in early spring, replanting into moist soil.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, damp soil in full sun, spacing them to allow the tall stems to arch freely. Keep the roots moist through summer for the best display. Drooping white bottlebrush tails hang from the stem tips from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Divide in spring and replant into fertile, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Site in full sun with steady moisture and give the clump space to reach its full height. The airy foliage forms a wide skirt beneath the flowering stems. Deep purple-red dangling tassels nod on tall arching stems in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into moist, fertile soil; a compact, front-of-border form.

directions: Set this dwarf selection near the front of a bed in sun and evenly moist soil. Its low mound of blue-green leaves stays tidy and needs no staking. Short dark burgundy button flowers stand upright on slim stems from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into moisture-retentive garden soil.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in soil kept reliably damp. The compact clump carries wiry stems well above the foliage. Small rosy-pink oval flower heads bob on the stem tips through mid and late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into rich, moist soil in an open position.

directions: Give this tall selection full sun and consistently moist, fertile soil. The see-through stems weave nicely among grasses and other prairie-style perennials. Numerous small dark red drumstick flowers hover on branching stems from mid summer into fall.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring into moist, well-worked soil.

directions: Plant in full sun to part shade in soil that stays evenly moist. The upright branching stems rarely need support and mix well in naturalistic plantings. Deep maroon-red oval flower heads open in mid to late summer and hold their color into autumn.

planting.note: Divide in spring, replanting into moisture-retentive fertile soil.

directions: Grow in full sun with steady moisture for the strongest stems. The tall, airy habit adds movement to a mixed border. Dark chocolate-brown to maroon flower heads dangle from slender stems in mid and late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, damp, humus-rich soil.

directions: Give this selection a cool root run in moist soil with light shade in hot climates. The grey-green ferny leaves form a low mound. Extra-long fuzzy lilac-pink tassels hang and curl like bushy tails from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into moist soil; a slow, compact variegated form.

directions: Site in light shade to protect the variegation from scorch, keeping the soil evenly moist. It grows slowly, so a front-of-border spot suits its size. Cream-edged leaves form a neat mound topped by small red-pink flower heads in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into fertile, moisture-retentive soil in full sun.

directions: Grow in full sun with reliable moisture for tall, self-supporting stems. Its transparent structure suits prairie and matrix-style plantings. Small white pom-pom flowers scatter on branching stems from mid summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into deep, moist, fertile soil.

directions: Give this vigorous, tall selection full sun and consistently moist ground with room to spread. The strong stems generally stand without staking. Large cranberry-red drooping flower heads open on branching stems in late summer and hold well into fall.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring into rich, moist soil in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun with steady moisture and allow generous space for its tall stature. The see-through flowering stems tower over the basal foliage. Dark maroon oblong flower heads sway on very tall branching stems from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide in spring and replant into moisture-retentive garden soil.

directions: Grow in full sun to part shade in evenly moist soil. The wiry branching stems carry many small flowers well above the leaves. Round dark red button heads stud the plant from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into fertile, moist soil in an open site.

directions: Site in full sun with consistent moisture for the tallest, sturdiest stems. Its airy form reads well through grasses and late-season perennials. Long, drooping pink bottlebrush spikes dangle from the stem tips in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in soil kept reliably moist. The slender branching stems weave through neighboring plants for a loose, natural look. Deep mulberry-red oval flower heads appear from mid summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into deep, moist, fertile soil in full sun.

directions: Give this very tall selection full sun, deep moist soil, and plenty of room. Despite its height the stems are unusually self-supporting and stand upright. Slim red-purple flower spikes rise on towering stems from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring into fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Grow in full sun with reliable moisture for strong upright stems. The transparent structure suits prairie-style and mixed borders. Small dusky-red drumstick flowers hover on branching stems from mid summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide in spring and replant into moist, well-worked soil.

directions: Plant in full sun and evenly moist soil for the earliest bloom. The blue-green scalloped foliage emerges as a neat clump in spring. Plump deep crimson flower heads open early, from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Sow or divide in spring into rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in fertile soil that stays damp. The finely cut leaves form a low base beneath the tall stems. Nodding white bottlebrush flower heads arch over the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in spring once the soil has warmed and the danger of hard frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover seed with a quarter inch of soil and keep the bed evenly moist until sprouts appear. Give this vigorous, rhizome-spreading plant room or a contained bed, since it colonizes freely. Expect clusters of fragrant pale-pink flowers from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring after the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before and water them in well. Because the fully double blooms are sterile, increase your stock by lifting and splitting clumps in spring or fall rather than by seed. Enjoy the frothy pompon-like pink flowers from midsummer onward, and shear back after bloom to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in ordinary, well-drained soil.

directions: Settle divisions into loosened soil and keep them watered until new growth resumes. Propagate this double-flowered form by division since it does not come true from seed. Creamy double-white flowers open in mid to late summer and are lightly fragrant in the evening.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring.

directions: Place divisions at their original depth in average garden soil and water regularly the first season. Increase this cultivar only by splitting clumps, as the sterile double flowers set no viable seed. The rosy-red double blooms deepen to a softer pink as they age, appearing through summer.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions level with the surrounding soil and firm them in gently. This vigorous double white selection spreads by runners, so give it space or edge the bed. Its full, scented white flowers cover the plant from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the cream-edged foliage can show.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil and remove any all-green shoots that appear to keep the variegation strong. Grown mainly for its green leaves margined in creamy white, it also produces single pink flowers in summer. Divide clumps in spring to propagate, since seedlings lose the leaf pattern.

planting.note: Sow in spring or start indoors, then set out in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface, as light aids germination, and keep the soil just moist. This trailing mat is ideal spilling over walls, rocks, and slopes where drainage is sharp. A carpet of bright rose-pink flowers appears in late spring; shear the plant back afterward to keep it compact.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press it in without burying, keeping the bed lightly moist until sprouts show. Use this low, spreading form to drape over rockwork and retaining walls in lean soil. Clouds of pure-white flowers cover the mat in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or rooted cuttings in spring in gritty soil.

directions: Set plants into fast-draining soil and avoid heavy feeding, which loosens the tight habit. This denser, deeper-colored selection is best increased from soft cuttings taken after flowering. Deep carmine-rose blooms smother the compact mat in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny border with good drainage.

directions: Plant divisions at their prior depth and cut the stems back hard in early spring to encourage fresh growth. This sterile hybrid is propagated by division or cuttings and blooms far longer than the species. Rosy-pink flowers appear from midsummer well into fall, especially if spent stems are trimmed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a raised rock garden or trough with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this compact hybrid in gritty alpine soil that never stays wet in winter. Increase it from cuttings, as the plant sets little or no viable seed. Large clear-pink flowers sit nearly stemless over a tight green cushion in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden or trough with excellent drainage.

directions: Site this dwarf cushion in lean, sharply drained soil and shelter it from winter wet. Propagate by taking small cuttings, since the plant rarely produces seed. Bright pink flowers cover the low green mound in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring; seed benefits from a period of cold before germinating.

directions: Sow into gritty alpine mix and give the seed a cold, moist spell to improve sprouting. Grow this tiny mountain cushion in a trough or scree where it stays cool and well drained. Nearly stemless magenta-pink flowers stud the mat in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in gritty, well-drained alpine soil.

directions: Start seed in a sharply drained mix and keep it cool and evenly moist until germination. This Pyrenean tuft-former suits rock gardens and troughs in full sun. Clusters of rose-pink flowers rise just above the cushion in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in gritty soil; a cold spell aids germination.

directions: Sow into a lean, well-drained mix and keep it cool until seedlings emerge. Rosettes of spoon-shaped basal leaves send up slender stems topped with rounded flower heads. Soft yellow to pale-pink flowers gather in tight clusters in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a warm, sunny, sharply drained site.

directions: Surface-sow into gritty soil in a warm spot and keep barely moist until germination. This Mediterranean species trails low and appreciates heat and excellent drainage. Deep pink to rose flowers appear over the spreading foliage in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Surface-sow in pots and give a cold winter outdoors, then set out rosettes in spring.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto gritty, lime-rich soil and leave it uncovered, as light aids sprouting. Grow the silver-edged rosettes in sharply drained scree where crowns stay dry through winter. Slender panicles of creamy white flowers rise above the mats in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for winter chilling, then plant rosettes into rock crevices in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of lean, gritty compost and keep it cool and lightly moist. Each broad silver rosette flowers only once, so allow offsets or seedlings to carry on the planting. Large arching plumes of white bloom appear in early summer before the parent rosette dies.

planting.note: Divide or plant the mossy cushions in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Set clumps into humus-rich, gritty soil in dappled shade where roots stay evenly cool. Water in dry spells, since the emerald hummocks scorch and brown out in hot, dry sun. Wiry stems carry white, pink, or red starry flowers over the moss-like mounds in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Peg down the runner plantlets or set divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant the rounded, silver-veined rosettes in cool, moist shade with good leaf-mould in the soil. It spreads freely by thin red runners that root down new plants, which you can lift and move at will. Airy sprays of small white flowers with unequal petals rise in early summer.

planting.note: Give seed a long cold period, then plant seedlings into cool scree in spring.

directions: Sow onto gritty, lime-rich mix and chill through winter, as this arctic plant germinates slowly. Grow the ground-hugging mats in cool, sharply drained soil that never bakes. Rosy-purple cup flowers stud the tiny-leaved cushions very early, often as snow retreats.

planting.note: Split and replant the spreading rosettes in spring or after flowering.

directions: Plant the leathery evergreen rosettes as edging or groundcover in cool part shade. It knits together into a tough, weed-smothering carpet and tolerates poor soil and neglect. Airy sprays of pale pink, red-dotted flowers hover on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn where plants are to grow in grass or borders.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of well-drained soil in autumn and let winter cold break dormancy. Plants grow from small bulbils and go dormant by midsummer, so pair them with later-emerging neighbors. Loose sprays of pure white five-petalled flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant or divide crowns in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot.

directions: Grow in cool, humus-rich woodland soil that stays reliably moist but never waterlogged. Shelter the fleshy, often bronze-red leaves from harsh sun and drying wind. Airy sprays of white, lopsided flowers appear late, from early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Sow in autumn and grow on for several years before the single flowering.

directions: Sow on gritty, lime-rich mix and be patient, as the symmetrical silver rosette takes years to mature. Grow it in a tight limestone crevice with excellent drainage and no offsets to spoil the single rosette. It flowers once with a magnificent arching plume of white bloom, then dies.

planting.note: Chill seed over winter, then plant seedlings into moist, gritty soil in spring.

directions: Unlike most saxifrages this one likes constant moisture, so plant it beside a stream or in wet scree. Set the fleshy, needle-leaved mats in gritty soil that never dries out. Starry yellow flowers, often orange-flecked, open through summer.

planting.note: Divide the mossy mats and replant in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Plant the fine green cushions in gritty, humus-rich soil in cool part shade. Keep the mats from drying out in summer, when hot sun can brown the foliage. Slender stems carry nodding white flowers above the moss in late spring.

planting.note: Detach rooted rosettes or plant young clumps in gritty spring soil.

directions: Grow the tight silver rosettes in a sunny crevice with sharp, lime-rich drainage. Keep water off the cushions in winter to prevent rot at the crowns. Reddish stems carry sprays of white flowers just above the domed rosettes in early summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets or clumps into gritty, alkaline soil in spring.

directions: Set the narrow-leaved silver rosettes in a hot, well-drained crevice with lime in the soil. Protect the crowns from winter wet, which is the main cause of failure. Long arching sprays of white flowers rise above the rosettes in early summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed over winter, then plant seedlings into cool scree.

directions: Sow on gritty mix and chill through winter, as this arctic-alpine needs cold to sprout. Grow the small green cushions in cool, sharply drained soil that stays moist but airy. Short stems bear small creamy-white flowers over the tufts in late spring.

planting.note: Divide or plant rosettes in spring in cool, shaded soil.

directions: Plant the small spoon-shaped rosettes in cool, humus-rich woodland soil. It spreads gently by short runners to form a low evergreen carpet in shade. Delicate sprays of tiny white flowers float on thin stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cushions into sharply drained, lime-rich soil in spring.

directions: Grow the hard, spiny silver cushion in gritty tufa or scree with perfect drainage. Shade it from the hottest sun and keep winter wet off the crown. Large solitary white flowers on short red stems appear very early, sometimes late winter.

planting.note: Set rooted rosettes into gritty, alkaline scree in spring.

directions: Plant the neat lime-encrusted cushions in tufa or gritty soil with sharp drainage. Keep the crown dry in winter and give light shade in the hottest gardens. Clustered white to pale pink flowers open on short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cushions into gritty scree in early spring.

directions: Grow the prickly, deep-green cushion in sharply drained gritty soil in a trough or crevice. Its needle-like leaves form a dense hummock that resents winter wet at the crown. Small clustered yellow flowers appear on short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant the spreading rosettes in spring.

directions: Plant the flat evergreen rosettes as groundcover in cool, moist part shade. It spreads steadily to make a dense mat and is a parent of the familiar London pride. Sprays of small pale pink, red-spotted flowers rise on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or plant divisions in moist, shaded spring soil.

directions: Grow in cool, moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade, ideally near water. The kidney-shaped leaves make a leafy clump that dislikes drying out. Loose airy panicles of small white, red-spotted flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then plant seedlings into cool, gritty scree.

directions: Sow on gritty mix and give a long winter chill to break dormancy. Grow the tight moss-like cushions in cool, sharply drained soil that stays lightly moist. Short stems bear small creamy-white, dotted flowers over the mats in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted rosettes into gritty, alkaline scree in spring.

directions: Grow the hard, lime-encrusted cushion in tufa or gritty soil with faultless drainage. Keep it out of hot afternoon sun and shield the crown from winter wet. Clustered white flowers, sometimes pink-flushed, open on short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets into a lime-rich crevice; the main rosette flowers only once.

directions: Grow the large silver rosette in sharply drained, lime-rich scree in a rock crevice. Remove side offsets if you want the main rosette to build to its full flowering size. It sends up a long arching cascade of white flowers in spring, then that rosette dies and offsets carry on.

planting.note: Plant rooted rosettes into gritty, lime-rich soil in spring.

directions: Set the flat green-silver rosettes in sharply drained scree with lime in the soil. Keep winter wet off the crowns, which rot in soggy conditions. Arching sprays carry white flowers heavily marked with deep red-pink spots in early summer.

planting.note: Plant or divide the mossy cushions in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Set the compact green cushions in humus-rich, gritty soil in cool part shade. Keep the mats moist through summer, since dry heat browns the foliage. Short stems carry rose-pink starry flowers over the moss in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant or divide the mossy cushions in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Grow the low green mounds in cool, moist, gritty soil with some afternoon shade. Water in dry spells to keep the mossy foliage from scorching. Deep rosy-red flowers stand on slender stems above the cushions in late spring.

planting.note: Plant or divide the mossy cushions in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Plant the emerald mossy mats in gritty, humus-rich soil in cool part shade. Keep roots evenly moist, as hot dry sun scorches the fine foliage. Wiry stems carry deep blood-red starry flowers above the cushions in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant or divide the mossy cushions in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Grow the neat green cushions in cool, gritty, humus-rich soil out of harsh sun. Water during dry weather to keep the moss-like foliage fresh and green. Slender stems bear pure white starry flowers over the mats in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant or divide the golden cushions in cool spring soil, out of hot sun.

directions: Grow this golden-leaved form in light shade, since full sun scorches the bright foliage. Keep the gritty, humus-rich soil cool and evenly moist all summer. The chartreuse-gold moss lights up shade and carries small white flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Detach rooted rosettes and plant into gritty, lime-rich soil in spring.

directions: Grow the compact lime-encrusted rosettes in sharply drained, gritty scree. Keep water off the tight silver cushions through winter to avoid rot. Short sprays of creamy-white flowers rise above the neat mats in early summer.
